planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost into dry, sandy, lean, well-drained desert soil in full sun.

directions: Parry's Marina is a delicate native annual legume of dry, sandy desert washes and open plains in the Mojave and Sonoran Deserts of California, Arizona, and adjacent Mexico. It produces slender, branching stems with tiny, pinnate, gland-dotted leaves and small but brilliant blue-violet to purple pea flowers with yellow centers from spring through summer; native desert bees are particularly attracted to the blooms. Direct sow into dry, sandy, lean, very well-drained soil in full sun after the last frost date; no soil amendment or irrigation is needed once seedlings are established. Parry's Marina self-sows in suitable desert garden settings and makes a charming, rarely cultivated native addition to California and Arizona desert wildflower mixes.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, rocky, calcareous, very well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Arizona Marina is a native perennial legume of dry, rocky, calcareous hills and canyon walls in Arizona, New Mexico, and adjacent northern Mexico. It produces woody-based, branching stems with small, pinnate, gland-dotted leaves and short racemes of relatively large blue-violet pea flowers marked with a striking yellow keel spot, blooming from summer through fall. Scarify seeds lightly before fall direct sow into dry, rocky, calcareous, very well-drained soil in full sun; no supplemental irrigation is needed once the plant is established. An ecologically appropriate and underused native legume, Arizona Marina is well suited to Chihuahuan Desert and Arizona xeriscape gardens where lean, alkaline soils and summer drought are the norm.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into very dry, lean, rocky or sandy, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; keep away from livestock.

directions: Purple Locoweed is a low-growing, silky-hairy native perennial of dry, open, rocky or sandy prairies and hills throughout the Great Plains, producing basal rosettes of pinnate, silver-hairy leaves and upright stems bearing dense, showy spikes of rose-pink to magenta-purple pea flowers in spring; it is among the most ornamental native prairie legumes in cultivation. CAUTION: all parts are toxic to livestock — cattle, horses, and sheep that graze it in quantity develop a neurological condition known as locoism. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into very dry, lean, rocky or sandy, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; germination can be variable, so sow generously. An excellent, deeply drought-tolerant native for xeriscape and prairie gardens in zones 3 through 8 wherever livestock access can be controlled.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall sowing into very well-drained, rocky or gravelly, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun in cool climates.

directions: Showy Locoweed is a remarkably beautiful native locoweed of dry, rocky alpine and subalpine slopes in the northern Rockies and northern Great Plains, producing unusually dense, silky-woolly silver foliage that makes the plant appear frosted even in midsummer. The brilliant blue-violet to rose-purple flower spikes appearing in early summer are among the most intensely colored wildflowers of the northern plains and mountains. CAUTION: like other locoweeds, it is toxic to livestock and should be sited away from grazing animals. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into very well-drained, rocky or gravelly, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; it performs best in cool, dry climates and is an outstanding rock garden and alpine garden native for zones 2 through 7.
sources: Flora of North America — Oxytropis splendens

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing in spring into dry, sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun.

directions: Coastal Indigo is a low, spreading native perennial with attractive silvery-hairy, pinnate foliage and clusters of brilliant rose-pink to coral-red pea flowers that continue from spring through fall, making it one of the longest-blooming native legumes of the southeastern coastal plain. It is native to dry, sandy, open ground, pine flatwoods, and roadsides from Georgia to Texas, and is notably more ground-hugging and spreading than most other Indigofera species. Scarify seeds before sowing in spring into dry, sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; amending or over-irrigating the soil will cause decline. An underused, exceptionally long-blooming native perennial for sandy, full-sun Gulf Coast gardens in zones 7 through 10, drought-tolerant once established.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database
